title: DOGE alumni raise $160M at $1.4B for military-cyber AI startup Cathedral
subtitle: Four ex-Department of Government Efficiency staffers land Andreessen Horowitz and Sequoia to build AI-driven offensive and defensive cyber tools for the U.S. military.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Cathedral, a stealth startup founded by former Department of Government Efficiency staffers Gavin Kliger, Luke Farritor, Marko Elez, and Jack Stein, closed a $160 million round at a $1.4 billion valuation on July 22, 2026. Andreessen Horowitz and Sequoia Capital co-led the round and both firms took board seats. The company plans to use AI to expand U.S. military cyber capabilities — both offensive operations against adversaries such as China and defensive protection of federal systems — and is either acquiring a data center or partnering with one to secure dedicated compute for those workloads.

The size of the round matters because it is the largest known raise by DOGE alumni to date, and it lands at the same valuation Palantir carried at its Series C. The founders spent much of 2025 firing federal workers and cutting agency budgets — more than 250,000 people left the federal workforce during that stretch — and are now redirecting the same access-and-speed playbook toward selling AI cyber tools back to the government they downsized. Investor appetite for the pitch, at a $1.4 billion valuation before any public product, tells you how much political and procurement access is being priced into the deal.

Cathedral fits a broader 2026 pattern of AI-native defense startups raising at unicorn-plus valuations without shipping public products — Helsing crossed $1.8 billion in Series E funding earlier this month, and Neo raised $100 million on July 21 for agentic software security. The defense-tech thesis is that legacy primes like Lockheed and Raytheon cannot ship modern autonomous systems fast enough, and that the next wave of Pentagon contracts will flow to smaller AI-first shops with founders who already know the procurement corridor. Anduril proved that thesis works; Cathedral is betting the same is true for offensive cyber.

For learners: watch who backs a defense-tech startup, not just how much they raise. A16z and Sequoia leading a $160 million round for founders with under two years of federal experience means the bet is on political access and speed of deployment, not on a proven technical moat. If you are choosing where to build an AI career, the money is telling you defense and government contracting are back in fashion after a decade in the wilderness — and that fluency in how the DoD, CIA, and Cyber Command actually buy software may be as valuable as any ML skill on your resume.

sources.title: DOGE alumni launch military cyber startup with $1.4 billion valuation — Reuters via US News
Andreessen Horowitz, Sequoia Capital Lead $160M Round for Former DOGE Staff's Venture — citybiz
Four DOGE alumni raise at a $1.4 billion valuation for Cathedral — Startup Fortune
